core_intuitions: Sparse per token, dense per batch: each token touches only k/E experts, but a modest decode batch's union touches ~85%+ of all experts, so every expert must stay resident even though any one token uses almost none of them (the memory inversion).

The all-to-all is a synchronization barrier: dispatch+combine complete only when the slowest, most-overloaded rank finishes, so a single hot expert taxes every GPU every decode step (the bottleneck inversion / imbalance factor).

On the decode critical path the all-to-all should be INVISIBLE: DeepEP's hook-based overlap hides RDMA behind shared-expert/attention compute, and this overlap is the entire difference between eta ~0.3 and eta ~0.9.

Expert-parallel mechanics = dispatch (all-to-all-v) -> local grouped GEMM -> combine (all-to-all-v, gate-weighted), which is a fundamentally different communication pattern than a dense model's fixed-size all-reduce.
The modern layout is hybrid: attention runs data-parallel (local KV, zero collective) while experts run expert-parallel on the SAME GPUs, so the MoE all-to-all is the only collective in the layer.
Prefill and decode want opposite EP configs (bandwidth vs latency; small EP vs large EP), which is the core argument for disaggregating them.

existing.name: moe-serving-dense-tp-vs-moe-ep
existing.issue: Side-by-side of dense-FFN-TP (one fixed all-reduce, well overlapped) vs MoE-FFN-EP (router -> dispatch all-to-all #1 -> local grouped GEMM -> combine all-to-all #2). Reads clearly in both themes; correctly contrasts 'one fixed-size collective' vs 'two data-dependent collectives, cost set by the busiest GPU'. Right-edge annotations sit close to the viewBox edge but remain fully legible. Serves the dispatch/compute/combine mechanics intuition well.
existing.improvement: Optional only: nudge the two right-side annotation captions ('send to owner GPUs / irregular sizes', 'gather back & gate-weighted sum') a few px left so they clear the panel border with more margin. Not required.

existing.name: moe-serving-attn-dp-expert-ep-layout
existing.issue: Shows the hybrid layout: rows of GPUs each holding LOCAL attention+KV (data-parallel, no replication, no collective) plus an expert shard (expert-parallel), joined by a vertical ALL-TO-ALL bus where tokens from all GPUs mix, alongside a per-MoE-layer sequence. Legible in both themes; the color legend (blue = attention/local, orange = experts/collective) is clear; the straggler/balance caption is on-message. This is the definitive figure for the attention-DP + expert-EP intuition.

new_figures.name: moe-serving-sparse-token-dense-batch
new_figures.concept: Each token lights up only k of E experts, but the union over a modest batch lights up nearly all of them -> every expert must be resident.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's FIRST inversion and its most counterintuitive load-bearing claim ('a batch of 64 touches ~87% of experts'). Nothing existing visualizes it. Without this, the reader cannot feel WHY 671B of weights must stay resident even though each token is sparse -- the entire memory framing of MoE serving rests on it.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Why MoE Serving Is a Different Problem', immediately after the sentence 'You cannot avoid keeping (almost) all experts resident and ready.'
new_figures.brief: Three-panel left-to-right infographic on the theme 'sparse per token, dense per batch'. LEFT panel titled 'One token': draw a single token node with k=2 arrows fanning to exactly 2 lit cells in a horizontal strip of E=8 expert cells (rest of the strip drawn as faint/unlit outlines). Small label 'touches k of E experts (k/E of the weights)'. MIDDLE panel titled 'A batch of a few tokens': stack 4-5 token rows, each row an independent strip of the same 8 expert cells with a different sparse pair lit (token1 lights {2,5}, token2 {1,5}, token3 {3,8}, token4 {2,7}, token5 {6,8} -- illustrative small integers, ensure overlaps so the union still misses maybe one cell). Then draw a bold UNION strip below them (an OR of all rows) where 7 of 8 cells are now lit; annotate 'union: almost every expert touched'. RIGHT panel titled 'Why: distinct experts vs batch size': a small schematic rising curve, x-axis 'batch size B', y-axis 'distinct experts touched' with a dashed horizontal asymptote labelled 'all E'. The curve rises steeply and plateaus near the asymptote; place a marker on the knee labelled 'even a modest decode batch -> ~all experts'. Do NOT print invented benchmark numbers on the axes; keep it schematic. Bottom caption spanning all panels: 'Each token is sparse, but the batch is nearly dense in expert coverage -> all experts must stay resident.' Theme-safe: use an accent fill for LIT cells and low-opacity stroke-only for UNLIT cells so light/dark both read; the union strip uses the strongest accent. Keep all cells and labels inside the viewBox; clear left-to-right reading order.

new_figures.name: moe-serving-hot-expert-barrier
new_figures.concept: The all-to-all completes only at the slowest rank; a hot expert makes one GPU a straggler and forces every other GPU to sit idle at the barrier -> imbalance factor IF = max/mean directly scales effective expert time.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls routing skew a 'tail-latency weapon' and devotes a full section plus the imbalance-factor formula to it, and it is the third 'inversion' (who is the bottleneck). The existing figures mention 'cost set by the busiest GPU' in captions but never VISUALIZE the barrier and the wasted idle time. Making 'everyone waits for the hot rank' visible is what converts IF from an equation into an intuition.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Hot-Expert Skew at Decode and Load Balancing', immediately after the imbalance-factor equation (IF = max L_g / mean L_g) and before the code block.
new_figures.brief: A single-frame 'barrier' infographic contrasting balanced vs skewed load across G=4 GPUs in an EP group. Main view: 4 vertical columns labelled GPU0..GPU3. Each column is a stack of small token blocks representing tokens that rank's experts must process this step. In the SKEWED case draw heights 3, 3, 3, 9 (illustrative small integers) so GPU3 is the hot straggler; color GPU3's tall stack in a warning accent, the others in the neutral/expert accent. Draw a horizontal dashed 'mean load' line across the three short bars (~mean 4.5). Draw a bold horizontal 'BARRIER: all-to-all completes here' line at the TOP of the tallest (GPU3) bar. Above each of the three short bars, up to the barrier line, fill a hatched 'idle / waiting' region and label one of them 'FLOPs wasted waiting'. Annotate 'IF = max/mean ~ 2  ->  ~half the aggregate expert FLOPs idle every step'. Provide a small INSET (upper corner or right side) titled 'Balanced (IF ~ 1)' showing 4 equal bars (~5,5,4,5) with the barrier line hugging their tops and almost no hatched idle -> visual proof that flattening the histogram removes the wait. Small caption: 'Because every decode step crosses the barrier, a hot expert taxes every step -- skew lives in the p99 tail.' Theme-safe: token blocks and mean line must read in both light/dark (use accent + neutral, hatched idle via low-opacity diagonal stroke, not a hardcoded gray fill). Keep bars, barrier line, inset, and labels fully inside the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading.

new_figures.name: moe-serving-alltoall-overlap-timeline
new_figures.concept: Serialized decode exposes the all-to-all (GPU idle inside every MoE layer) whereas DeepEP hook-based overlap runs RDMA on the NIC lane while the SM lane computes shared-expert/attention -> the exposed all-to-all shrinks toward zero and wall-clock per layer drops.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter names this 'the single most important idea' and says the overlap is literally the difference between eta ~0.3 and eta ~0.9. It is inherently temporal -- a pipeline where communication is hidden behind compute while a different micro-batch is in flight -- which a static frame can show but motion makes visceral. This is the payoff mechanism of the whole chapter and is not covered by either existing figure.
new_figures.placement: Section 'DeepEP: A Production All-to-All Kernel', after the paragraph describing the low-latency hook-based overlap and 'communication and computation truly overlap', near the code sketch.
new_figures.brief: Two stacked Gantt-style timelines for ONE MoE decode layer, sharing a left-to-right time axis, showing the same total work but different wall-clock. TOP timeline titled 'Serialized (naive all_to_all)': a single 'SM / compute' lane running the sequence [router] -> [DISPATCH all-to-all: lane goes IDLE, hatched] -> [expert grouped GEMM] -> [COMBINE all-to-all: lane IDLE, hatched] -> end. Mark the two hatched idle blocks 'exposed all-to-all -> GPU idle'. Show total wall-clock as a long bracket labelled 'high TPOT, low eta'. BOTTOM timeline titled 'Overlapped (DeepEP low-latency hook)': TWO lanes -- an upper 'SM / compute' lane and a lower 'NIC / RDMA' lane. Sequence: [router] on compute lane; then dispatch RDMA starts on the NIC lane (a token block travels along the NIC lane) WHILE the compute lane simultaneously runs 'shared-expert FFN' and 'next micro-batch attention' (busy, not idle); a small 'hook()' marker where the compute lane finally waits only at arrival; then [expert grouped GEMM] on compute lane while combine RDMA overlaps on the NIC lane. Compute lane stays busy end-to-end; NIC lane runs underneath. Show the shorter total wall-clock bracket labelled 'low exposed all-to-all -> high eta'. Include a tiny legend: compute lane = expert accent, comm lane = a second accent, idle = hatched. ANIMATION (long-animation): a playhead sweeps left->right across both timelines in lockstep; in the top timeline the compute lane visibly greys/hatches during the two all-to-all blocks; in the bottom timeline a token block animates traveling along the NIC lane (in flight) while the compute lane fills with shared-expert/attention work, and the 'hook()' marker snaps at the moment of arrival; loop. The frozen first frame must already show both full timelines with the idle-vs-busy contrast so it is understandable without playing. Theme-safe: no hardcoded primary colors, use two accents plus hatched idle that read in light and dark; do not print invented eta numbers on the bars (the 'low/high eta' labels are qualitative). Keep both timelines, lanes, and labels inside the viewBox.

summary: Chapter is concept-rich and currently has two strong, correct figures that both survive audit as KEEP in light and dark themes: the dense-TP-vs-MoE-EP mechanics contrast and the attention-DP + expert-EP hybrid layout. Those cover the dispatch/compute/combine mechanics (inversion 2) and the modern layout. The gaps are the chapter's other two 'inversions' plus its stated single most important idea. I propose three new figures, each foundational and non-redundant: (1) 'sparse per token, dense per batch' -- the memory inversion that justifies keeping all experts resident (priority 1, static); (2) the hot-expert all-to-all BARRIER with wasted idle and the imbalance factor -- the bottleneck inversion and tail-latency weapon (priority 2, static); and (3) the DeepEP overlap timeline, serialized-vs-overlapped, which the chapter calls the single most important idea and the eta 0.3->0.9 lever (priority 1, long-animation because the in-flight-while-computing pipeline is genuinely temporal). I deliberately did NOT add figures for the prefill/decode asymmetry (already well served by the comparison table) or the economics eta formula (a clean equation), to stay disciplined.
